senses.gloss: A contraction of us. Only in let's.
senses.example: Let's go to the store; I'm so hungry!

senses.gloss: You use / to mean as well as or and, used to separate two items in a list.
senses.example: She's the cook / waitress.

senses.gloss: A is used when you don't expect your listener to identify which thing you mean.
senses.example: Compare "A book I saw on the shelf" and "The book I gave you yesterday".

senses.gloss: Enough of a particular quality to be noticed.
senses.example: Her job gave her a certain freedom.

senses.gloss: Definite or particular, but not named or specified.
senses.example: A certain person called on me yesterday.

senses.gloss: a small number
senses.example: I saw him only a few days ago.

senses.gloss: à la refers to being according to, or in the style of something.
senses.example: I hadn't cooked tomatoes à la crème in a long time.

senses.gloss: a small amount
senses.example: We've only got a little time, so please be quick.

senses.gloss: When something is a priori, it refers to things you cannot know in advance. You have to try first and then find the answer.
a.m. is used after a number to show that you are referring to a particular time between midnight to midday.
senses.example: The program starts at 9 a.m.

senses.gloss: toward the back end of a ship
on or onto a boat, airplane, train, etc.
senses.example: When everyone is aboard, the doors will close.

senses.gloss: You use about to say what subject something is related to.
senses.example: Let's not talk about her problems.

senses.gloss: If something moves about, it moves in many directions.
senses.example: He walked about the place, looking everywhere.

senses.gloss: Having to do with, concerning, regarding.
senses.example: I will talk about dogs.

senses.gloss: Nearly, approximately.
senses.example: They're about the same.

senses.gloss: If A is above B, A is higher than or before B, but not touching B.
senses.example: In a newspaper, the title of a story is usually above the story.

senses.gloss: The above comment, discussion, argument, etc. is one higher than or coming before something.
senses.example: Please see the above discussion of the problem.

senses.gloss: If something or someone is aboveboard, they are honest.
If something is done aboveboard, it is done in an honest way.
If you keep or stay abreast of something, you regularly read or study the most recent information about it.
senses.example: Have you been keeping abreast of the news from Iraq?

senses.gloss: If two things are abreast, they are side by side.
senses.example: They walked in a long line, three abreast.

senses.gloss: If somebody goes abroad, they leave their home country and go to another country.
senses.example: They sent their children to school abroad.

senses.gloss: If somebody lives abroad, they live in another country.
senses.example: She spent four years abroad before returning to Brazil.

senses.gloss: Absent means without or barring.
senses.example: Absent any problems, we should be finished in two hours.

senses.gloss: If something is true according to a source, it means that source says or shows that it is true.
senses.example: According to a friend of mine, they are thinking of building a new mall near here.

senses.gloss: To the other side.
senses.example: I walked across the bridge.

senses.gloss: On the other side.
senses.example: I can see him across the street.

senses.gloss: Crossing, on both sides of.
senses.example: The bridge is across the stream.

senses.gloss: If x is adjacent to y, there is no other thing between x and y.
senses.example: The cinema was immediately adjacent to our house, so that every night we could hear the film.

senses.gloss: If something is adrift, it is floating with no control of its direction.
senses.example: The boat was adrift for three days before it was found with nobody aboard.

senses.gloss: If something is afloat, it is on the water; floating.
senses.example: Although he couldn't swim, the life jacket kept him afloat.

senses.gloss: At, close to, or towards the rear of a boat or plane.
senses.example: They spent their evening on the ship's deck. One stood fore, the other aft.

senses.gloss: Behind something in place or position.
senses.example: "B" comes after "A" in the alphabet.

senses.gloss: Later in time.
senses.example: He came home after Jane did.

senses.gloss: If something happens afterward, it happens after the time that you are talking about.
senses.example: Joe went to the bank. Afterward he went to the store.

senses.gloss: If something happens afterwards, it happens after the time that you are talking about.
Once more.
senses.example: They didn't understand the answer, so they asked again.

senses.gloss: Opposing or disagreeing with sb/sth.
senses.example: I am against that proposal. I think it is a bad idea.

senses.gloss: Pressed to.
senses.example: He held her head against his chest.

senses.gloss: At an earlier time.
senses.example: I was there ten years ago.

tests.result: obligatory; a modifier, not a complement
not modifiable by -ly adverb placement typical of adverbs

override.notes: Intransitive, with an obligatory pre-head measure modifier (ten years ago; cf. ten years before I went). Not postposed: contrast notwithstanding, whose preposed NP is a complement

senses.gloss: If a boat runs or goes aground, its bottom goes onto the ground and it is not floating freely anymore.
senses.example: The boat ran aground off the coast of Florida and had to be helped to shore.

senses.gloss: You use ahead to talk about something that is in front of you or in the future.
You use albeit to give more information that weakens earlier information.
senses.example: He won the fight, albeit barely.

senses.gloss: All of something is 100% of it, or the entire amount.
senses.example: Well, you could stay in bed all day on Friday. And I could take the kids to school.

senses.gloss: All of some things is 100% of them, or every one.
senses.example: And then you can go and tell all your friends about it.

senses.gloss: You use allowing for something, to show that you have thought about that thing in making your decision or getting your answer.
senses.example: Even allowing for inflation, $3.7 million is too big a change.

senses.gloss: up in the air; flying
senses.example: He walked into the dark room, holding his light aloft.

senses.gloss: If you do something alone, you do it without other people.
senses.example: She went walking alone because she wanted to think.

senses.gloss: When you talk about something alone, you mean only that thing.
senses.example: It will take weeks for the planning alone.

senses.gloss: If one thing happens along with another, they go together.
senses.example: Along with his daughter, he is studying French.

senses.gloss: If you go along with something, you agree with or follow it.
senses.example: We're going to go along with your plan.

senses.gloss: You use along to show movement from one end of a long thing towards the other end.
senses.example: Along the road were just a few cars.

senses.gloss: If something was true all along, it's true from the beginning.
senses.example: He knew the real story all along.

senses.gloss: You use along with to add more information.
senses.example: We keep both the old ideas along with the new ones.

senses.gloss: You use alongside to talk about something being beside something else.
You use alongside to talk about something being at the same time as something else.
You use although to show that an idea is surprising or different from another idea.
senses.example: We decided to take rooms in Longwood house, although we knew we could not really afford the rent there.

senses.gloss: You use amid to talk about something happening at the same time as many other things.
senses.example: Amid all the riots during the blackout, many stores were looted.

senses.gloss: You use amid to talk about something being in the middle of something else.
If something is amidst other things, it is in the middle of them.
senses.example: She saw the moon rising above the open sea amidst flying clouds.

senses.gloss: If something happens amidst other things, it happens at the same time as them.
senses.example: Amdist all the packing and moving, she still had to study.

senses.gloss: In or into. We say "between" about 2 other things, but among about 3 or more other things.
senses.example: She was among her friends.

senses.gloss: With a share for each.
senses.example: Divide the candy among yourselves.

senses.gloss: By all or with the whole.
In or into. We say "between" 2 other things, but amongst 3 or more other things.
senses.example: She was amongst her friends.

senses.gloss: With a share for each.
senses.example: Divide the candy amongst yourselves.

senses.gloss: By all or with the whole.
An is used when you don't expect your listener to identify which thing you mean.
senses.example: I just ate an apple.

senses.gloss: You use and to talk about two things at once.
senses.example: I like singing and reading.

senses.gloss: You use and when you are listing a few things and you are now on your last item of the list.
senses.example: I like singing, reading, cycling and playing soccer.

senses.gloss: And is used when you are putting some sentences together.
senses.example: She came into the store, shouted at the cashier, and left.

senses.gloss: Used to show what happened after something else.
senses.example: The alarm went off and I woke up.

tests.result: links elements of equal syntactic status; no subordination diagnostics

senses.gloss: You use and/or when listing things where either or both of them are options. It combines the meaning of the words "and" and "or".
senses.example: Please list three business and/or personal references.

senses.gloss: Another of something means that there is one more of it.
senses.example: Is there another way to do this?

senses.gloss: before
Usually abbreviated to a.m., an ante meridiem time is in the morning, i.e., before noon.
senses.example: The work day here formally begins at 8:00 ante meridiem.

senses.gloss: Against.
senses.example: I'm anti the idea of moving the couch there. I think that's a horrible place for it.

senses.gloss: some or even the smallest amount or number
senses.example: Do you want any coffee?

senses.gloss: it does not matter which
senses.example: Please ask me. I can answer any question.

senses.gloss: Any one person out of a number of people.
senses.example: I don't care who comes. Anybody will do.

senses.gloss: Any person. You use this word when there is no limit to who might be meant. You might be able to change anyone to any person's name.
senses.example: Help! Can anyone hear me?

senses.gloss: Any one of all things. You use this when there is no limit to what might be meant. You might be able to change "anything" to any other word.
senses.example: I'll do anything you want.

senses.gloss: Anytime is any time at all.
senses.example: I have no plans this weekend; I can paint the house anytime.

senses.gloss: (in, at or to) any place
senses.example: I don't have anywhere to go.

senses.gloss: If two things are apart, there is space between them.
senses.example: Scientists theorize that Pangaea drifted apart into the different continents that exist on Earth today.

senses.gloss: You use something apart to leave that thing out of the topic.
senses.example: The lies apart, they're good workers.

senses.gloss: in abundance
senses.example: While there are amenities aplenty, what makes Surf Simply stand out is the coaching and education.

senses.gloss: related to (a topic)
senses.example: "My toe is cold," he said, apropos of nothing at all.

senses.gloss: If x is around y, y is in the middle and x is on all sides of y.
senses.example: The moon circles around the Earth.

senses.gloss: A word that is used to compare two things that are equal.
senses.example: As you know, we need more workers.

senses.gloss: A word that is used to show that two things happened at the same time.
senses.example: Just as I went out, it started to rain.

senses.gloss: A word that is used to show why something happens.
senses.example: As I couldn't understand French, I didn't watch the film.

senses.gloss: A word that is used to express concession.
senses.example: Interesting as it seems, I don't think I'll get it.

senses.gloss: with respect to; in reference to; regarding
Used to show the time or date when something begins.
senses.example: As from November we will be moving to a new location.

senses.gloss: In the same way that it would be if.
senses.example: It could look as if we're trying to hide something.

senses.gloss: Used in exclamations to say that something is not true.
senses.example: "I'm not going to help anymore." "As if you were helping before!"

senses.gloss: if
senses.example: As long as you know what you're doing, it should be fine.

senses.gloss: Used to show the time or date when something begins or ends.
senses.example: As of November we will be moving to a new location.

senses.gloss: in the way that has been decided; following instructions, rules, etc.
senses.example: As per your instructions, I left the note on his desk.

senses.gloss: as (usual)
senses.example: As per usual, she arrived at 7:00.

senses.gloss: beginning at the time with no wait
senses.example: As soon as she heard the news, she began screaming.

senses.gloss: the same way that it would be if
senses.example: It could look as though we're trying to hide something.

senses.gloss: You use as to to identify the topic.
senses.example: They were not clear as to when it would end.

senses.gloss: You use as well as to add more information.
senses.example: He uses his web page to advertise his products as well as provide information about his hobby.

senses.gloss: on or onto the shore
senses.example: He came ashore after five weeks at sea.

senses.gloss: If you put something aside, you put it to the side, often because you want to use it later.
senses.example: We put some money aside for the children's education.

senses.gloss: You use something aside to leave that thing out of the topic.
senses.example: The lies aside, they're good workers.

senses.gloss: You use assuming (that) to say what would happen if something is true.
senses.example: That's good, assuming it's works.

senses.gloss: Used to say where something is, or when saying something's position
senses.example: I am at home.

senses.gloss: Used to say the time at which something happened or will happen
senses.example: Breakfast is at 9 o'clock.

senses.gloss: Used to direct attention towards a state, event, or detail
senses.example: Look at how lucky we are.

senses.gloss: Used to say something's state
senses.example: At half price!

senses.gloss: If x is atop y, x is on the highest part of y.
senses.example: He stood atop the mountain and looked all around.

senses.gloss: When something moves away from something else, the distance between them gets bigger. They are no longer near or close.
senses.example: Go away! You're bothering me.

senses.gloss: If something goes awry, it doesn't work as it's supposed to.
senses.example: Unfortunately, it looks like our plans have gone awry.

senses.gloss: Toward the rear.
senses.example: He went back behind the stands.

senses.gloss: To a place again.
senses.example: I didn't like it, so I sent it back.

senses.gloss: In the area in a theatre behind the stage.
senses.example: I met the stars backstage after the show.

senses.gloss: If something is backward, it is facing the back.
If something is backwards, it is facing the back.
except; not including
senses.example: He's the fastest runner in the world bar none.

senses.gloss: unless something happens or is true.
senses.example: Barring a big change in popular opinion, he should become the next president.

senses.gloss: You use because to show the reason for something.
senses.example: I bought the book because I wanted to read it.

tests.result: takes declarative clause or of-PP complement

override.notes: Traditional subordinating conjunction; CGEL analyses it as a preposition taking a clause complement

senses.gloss: If x happens before y, x happens first and y happens second.
senses.example: He worked in a gas station before he became famous.

senses.gloss: in front of something
senses.example: She stood before him, looking into his eyes.

senses.gloss: until
senses.example: It took a few moments before I realized that she was joking.

senses.gloss: Before something else happens.
senses.example: You should have told me about the presentation beforehand. I haven't even prepared.

senses.gloss: When something is behind a second thing, the first thing is in back of the second.
senses.example: The ball is behind the box.

senses.gloss: When something is behind, it is toward the rear.
senses.example: The man stayed behind.

senses.gloss: A thing is behind when it happened in the past.
senses.example: We left that worry behind.

senses.gloss: You use being to show the reason for something.
senses.example: The mother is very happy, being that her son has become a college student.

senses.gloss: At a lower position
At a lower position.
senses.example: The book is beneath the table.

senses.gloss: If x is beside y, it is close to y, but not in front or behind.
senses.example: The book is beside the table.

senses.gloss: In addition (to); as well (as).
senses.example: We've got a pile of homework and a part time job besides.

senses.gloss: Other than; except for.
senses.example: I don't want to go anywhere besides China.

senses.gloss: If x is between y and z, y is on one side of x and z is on the other side.
senses.example: Georgia is between North Carolina and Florida.

senses.gloss: between
If x is beyond y, it is on the far side of y.
senses.example: He went beyond the mountains, into the woods.

senses.gloss: ordinal billionth) The number 1,000,000,000 (or 1,000,000,000,000 in old British usage).
senses.example: In 2006, China had about 1.3 billion people.

senses.gloss: When there are two things, both is the first thing and the second thing.
senses.example: Jim took both the apple and the orange.

senses.gloss: You use but to join two ideas and show that they are not the same.
senses.example: I would take you there, but I do not have my car.

senses.gloss: You use but to change the topic.
senses.example: I understand. And I'm sorry. But there is nothing I can do about it now.

senses.gloss: except
senses.example: He ate everything but the meat.

senses.gloss: near or next to.
senses.example: The mailbox is by the bus stop.

senses.gloss: at some time before (the given time).
senses.example: Be back by ten o'clock!

senses.gloss: if something is done by somebody, it is done through their action
senses.example: The matter was decided by the chairman.

senses.gloss: if something is created by somebody, they are the creator, author, etc.
senses.example: There are many well-known plays by William Shakespeare.

senses.gloss: If x happens or is true by virtue of y, x is because of y.
senses.example: He became popular by virtue of the way he handled the issues.

senses.gloss: Moving in the direction of or facing the ceiling.
moving or facing the ceiling.
The number 10³⁰³.
Used to talk about groups or individuals without saying exactly which ones.
senses.example: Certain problems can be solved with very simple maths.

senses.gloss: At the home of.
senses.example: The painting remains chez Catherine and Stephane De Beyrie.

senses.gloss: on or around a particular date
senses.example: The city was built circa 1200 BCE.

senses.gloss: Out of, away from, especially too far away to be noticed or caught.
senses.example: After a short walk, we were clear of the town.

senses.gloss: When two things are close, they are near each other; the distance or time between them is small.
senses.example: The bank is close to the store.

senses.gloss: The comparative form of close; more close.
The superlative form of close; most close.
When something happens or arrives.
senses.example: We'll talk again come January.

senses.gloss: Compared to is used to show the difference between two things.
senses.example: Compared to the ideas of previous generations, there is a shift in the theory.

senses.gloss: about or relating to
senses.example: Concerning the schedule, there have been a number of changes.

senses.gloss: If x is consequent on y, x happens as a result of y.
senses.example: The flooding of the river was consequent on the heavy rain.

senses.gloss: When you think about; because of
senses.example: Considering all the problems we've had, it's amazing we finished.

senses.gloss: against; in opposition or contrast to
senses.example: Consider the problems of the nurse contra those of the doctor.

senses.gloss: If an act or idea is contrary to something, it is against it.
senses.example: The president's actions are contrary to the constitution and therefore illegal.

senses.gloss: You use contrary to x when x is different from the truth.
senses.example: Contrary to popular belief, the oil companies cannot control the price of crude.

senses.gloss: If x is counter to y, it is against y.
Including; with this/these included
senses.example: Counting the dog, we are a family of five.

senses.gloss: Coupled with is used to add a second part to a point.
senses.example: The team's win, coupled with a loss by their rivals, put them in first place.

senses.gloss: Cum is another word of "combine with".
Depending on x, means that the truth of what you say is affected by x.
senses.example: People in the rest of the country celebrated independence even later, depending on when the news of Congress's action reached them.

senses.gloss: If x happens despite y, it is surprising that x happens because y usually stops it.
senses.example: Despite their best efforts, the ministers have not been able to come to an agreement.

senses.gloss: If you do something despite yourself, you didn't plan or want to do it.
senses.example: She hated him, but when he spoke she laughed despite herself.

senses.gloss: right after; as soon as.
senses.example: I'll pay him a visit directly I've finished my tea.

senses.gloss: Toward the earth, away from the sky.
senses.example: What goes up on earth must fall down.

senses.gloss: Toward the bottom.
Towards a smaller price, number, amount, etc.
senses.example: I'm going to buy a computer once prices go down.

senses.gloss: If something is down under, it is in Australia.
senses.example: Surfing is a popular sport down under.

senses.gloss: If you go down under, you go to Australia.
senses.example: We traveled down under for our vacation.

senses.gloss: From higher ground to lower ground
senses.example: The bus rolled slowly downhill.

senses.gloss: If something is downstage from something else, it happens on towards or near the front of the stage in a theatre.
senses.example: The actors downstage were easier to hear.

senses.gloss: If something is downstairs, it is on the level at the bottom of the stairs.
senses.example: Let's head downstairs, from the bedroom to the kitchen below.

senses.gloss: If x is downstream from y, x and y are on or beside a river and the water moves from y to x.
senses.example: London is downstream from Oxford on the River Thames.

senses.gloss: In or in the direction of the center of a city or town.
senses.example: The bus is headed downtown.

senses.gloss: Outside the three-point line, or generally far from the basket.
senses.example: He took a shot from way downtown!

senses.gloss: moving or facing down
senses.example: The company's sales have been following a downward trend for the last three years.

senses.gloss: moving or facing down
senses.example: The company's sales have been following a downwards trend for the last three years.

senses.gloss: If x is downwind of y, the air is moving from y to x.
senses.example: Our house was downwind of the factory and the smell was terrible.

senses.gloss: If x is due to y, y causes x.
senses.example: Due to the recent changes, we were not able to complete this in time.

senses.example: He is twenty-three years old.

senses.gloss: ordinal: twenty-second) The number 22.
senses.example: He is going to turn twenty-two tomorrow.

senses.gloss: If something is done twice, it is done two times.
senses.example: I have been to the shop twice today.

senses.gloss: Two times: double.
senses.example: He was working twice as fast as I was.

senses.gloss: ordinal second) The number 2. Two means more than one and less than three.
senses.example: We have two eyes and two ears.

senses.gloss: ordinal two thousandth) Two thousand (2000) is a number that has two thousands.
senses.example: The number two thousand can be written as "2000".

senses.gloss: Umpteen is a large number of something; more than some.
senses.example: What have you been doing for the last umpteen days?

senses.gloss: If something is unbeknown to somebody, they don't know about it.
senses.example: Unbeknown to his wife, he had bought a new car.

senses.gloss: If something is unbeknownst to somebody, they don't know about it.
senses.example: Unbeknownst to his wife, he had bought a new car.

senses.gloss: In the same place as another thing, but lower.
senses.example: They ate under the trees.

senses.gloss: In the control of.
senses.example: He was under the leader of the organization.

senses.gloss: In the middle of a process; experiencing.
senses.example: The idea is under the committee's judgement (judging).

senses.gloss: under your feet; on the ground
senses.example: The snow underfoot crunched as we walked.

senses.gloss: being or moving under the ground
senses.example: Let's go underground. It's safer down there.

senses.gloss: If you go underground, you keep your activities secret.
If x is underneath y, x is right under, below, or covered by y.
senses.example: The cat was hiding underneath the car.

senses.gloss: If you say that x is true unless y, x is true only if y does not happen or is not true.
senses.example: I'll be there at 8:30 unless the train is late.

senses.gloss: not the same as; different from.
senses.example: This is unlike anything I have ever done before.

senses.gloss: If something is true until x, it is true for some time before x and then is not true.
senses.example: We didn't have any snow until the end of December.

senses.gloss: Directed or moving upwards.
senses.example: The flag is up.

senses.gloss: Toward the top or away from the Earth.
senses.example: The balloon went up.

senses.gloss: If a task or result is up to you, it means you are in charge of deciding or doing it.
senses.example: The outcome of the game is up to the players.

senses.gloss: If you are up to something, you are doing or planning it.
senses.example: What are you up to this evening?

senses.gloss: From lower ground to higher ground
senses.example: She got tired walking uphill.

senses.gloss: on or onto
senses.example: The police were called upon to save him.

senses.gloss: going to happen
senses.example: Christmas is almost upon us.

senses.gloss: at (a time)
senses.example: Upon his death, she became the owner of the store.

senses.gloss: toward or at the back of a stage.
senses.example: The actor turned and walked upstage.

senses.gloss: away from a camera.
If something is upstairs, it is on the level at the top of the stairs.
senses.example: Let us head upstairs, from the kitchen to the bedroom.

senses.gloss: If x is upstream from y, x and y are on or beside a river and the water moves from x to y.
senses.example: Oxford is upstream from London on the River Thames.

senses.gloss: moving or facing up
senses.example: The company's sales have been following an upward trend for the last three years.

senses.gloss: moving or facing up
senses.example: The company's sales have been following an upwards trend for the last three years.

senses.gloss: If x is upwind of y, the air is moving from x to y.
senses.example: I'd drop the boat slightly upwind of him so it would blow downwind to him.

senses.gloss: You use us to refer to yourself and other people together with you.
senses.example: It's OK for you guys, but it's different for us women.

senses.gloss: You use us to talk or write about yourself and other people together with you.
senses.example: She said hello to us.

senses.gloss: You use us all two emphasize that you are talking to or about 100% of the people with you.
senses.example: We have to make this better for us all.

senses.gloss: You use us both to emphasize that you are talking about yourself and another person together.
senses.example: It would be better for us both to go?

senses.gloss: A short way of saying versus.
You use various to identify more than one thing without saying exactly which ones.
senses.example: Various books have been taken.

senses.gloss: When we say one thing versus another thing, it means the two things are on different sides, going in different directions, or against each other.
senses.example: Tonight's game has Toronto versus Montreal.

senses.gloss: You use via to mean the path that something takes when it goes somewhere (the places it goes through before it gets there).
senses.example: We can send video, audio and data from any point in the UK, via satellite.

senses.gloss: in relation to; compared with
senses.example: Women still earn less money vis-à-vis men.

senses.gloss: facing, opposite, face to face with
senses.example: We stood vis-à-vis the beautiful painting.

senses.gloss: A short way of saying versus.
senses.example: The first wrestling match is Jones vs Smith.

senses.gloss: A short way of saying versus.
without
senses.example: I've got a right shoe wanting a left.

senses.gloss: The person speaking and other people with him or her.
senses.example: We Canadians don't act that way.

senses.gloss: The person speaking and other people with him or her.
senses.example: "Are you and Mia still going?" "No, we're finished."

senses.gloss: People in general.
senses.example: We need to think about other people more.

senses.gloss: One of the four compass directions: 270º clockwise from north. The sun sets in the west.
senses.example: England is west of France.

senses.gloss: Moving or facing west.
moving or facing west.
What is used to ask about the member(s) of a group.

senses.gloss: What is used to talk about the member(s) of a group.
senses.example: What gifts you can give are appreciated.

senses.gloss: What is used to ask for information about things.
senses.example: What did you see?

senses.gloss: What is used to add a clause with more information.
senses.example: I don't know what you mean.

senses.gloss: The thing(s) which: a small amount of something.
senses.example: Take what you can find.

senses.gloss: You use whatever when it doesn't matter which thing.

senses.gloss: You use whatever when there's a particular thing, but you can't remember what it was.

senses.gloss: You use whatever to make what stronger.

senses.gloss: You use whatever when it doesn't matter which thing.

senses.gloss: You use whatever when it doesn't matter which thing.
senses.example: Whatever you do will be OK.

senses.gloss: You use whatever when there's a particular thing, but you can't remember what it was.
senses.example: We took whatever came first.

senses.gloss: You use whatever to make what stronger.

senses.gloss: Whatever.
senses.example: Most studies find no relationship whatsoever.

senses.gloss: at what time; on what occasion
senses.example: "When did you start learning English?" – "Three years ago."

senses.gloss: at the time
senses.example: She phoned me when I was in the bath.

senses.gloss: after
senses.example: When I get up, I always drink a cup of coffee.

senses.gloss: from where
senses.example: Whence did you come?

senses.gloss: every time
senses.example: I practice whenever I have some free time.

senses.gloss: at any time
senses.example: You should call her whenever you can.

senses.gloss: (In or to) what place or situation.
senses.example: Where do you live?

senses.gloss: (In or to) the place or situation in which.
senses.example: The room where the people cook the food is not cold.

senses.gloss: You use where to introduce a clause that contrasts with the other parts of the sentence.
senses.example: Where Nixon was secretive and suspicious, Ford was relaxed, open and candid.

senses.gloss: Used to ask in what general place somebody or something is.
senses.example: Whereabouts do you come from in Korea?

senses.gloss: immediately after which
senses.example: She saw him, whereafter she began running.

senses.gloss: You use whereas to show a contrast between two things or ideas.
senses.example: Many kinds of roses have little or no scent, whereas others are well known for their beautiful smell.

senses.gloss: Because.
by which; because of which
senses.example: This hammer is the tool whereby I hammered in that nail.

senses.gloss: in which place
senses.example: She picked up the book wherein the paper was folded.

senses.gloss: immediately after which
senses.example: She saw him, whereupon she began running.

senses.gloss: A place.

senses.gloss: Any place.
senses.example: Wherever you go, I'll be there.

senses.gloss: Used to show surprise in a question.

senses.gloss: Whether is used to report questions and things that are unknown.

senses.gloss: Whether or not is used with a following clause when the truth of that clause does not matter or is in question.
senses.example: I'll go whether or not you do.

senses.gloss: You use which to ask for the selection of one or more members of some set.
senses.example: There are a lot of new books this year. Which ones do you like?

senses.gloss: You use which to add a clause to explain what thing you're talking about.

senses.gloss: You use which to add a clause to give more information about the thing you're talking about.

senses.gloss: You use whichever when it doesn't matter which thing.

senses.gloss: You use whichever when it doesn't matter which thing.

senses.gloss: If x happens while y happens, they happen at the same time.
senses.example: He fell asleep while sitting at his desk.

senses.gloss: Whilst is the synoym of while.
You use whither to mean to where, or to what place.
senses.example: Whither was the bus taking me?

senses.gloss: You use whither to say where something is going or has gone to.
senses.example: They worked in Toronto whither they had moved after leaving Montreal.

senses.gloss: You use who to add a clause to explain more about the person or people you're talking about.
senses.example: They are good people who are working hard.

senses.gloss: You use who to ask about a person or people.
senses.example: Who was on the phone?

senses.gloss: Whatever person or persons.
senses.example: Whoever thought up that stupid idea?

senses.gloss: Whatever person or persons.
senses.example: Whoever violates the law will be punished.

senses.gloss: Whom is the object form of who.

senses.gloss: Whom is the object form of who.
senses.example: I have a sister, whom I wrote to last week.

senses.gloss: Whose is used to ask what person something belongs to.
senses.example: Whose is this? There's no name on it.

senses.gloss: Whose is used to join a relative clause about a person or thing.
senses.example: Children whose parents smoke are more likely to smoke themselves.

senses.gloss: Whichever person; whoever.
senses.example: Whosoever comes back first will be the winner.

senses.gloss: If something goes wide of the target, it misses the target.
senses.example: The soccer ball went wide of the goal.

senses.gloss: If the ball or a player is/plays/goes wide, they are near the sides of the field or court.
senses.example: He ran wide and got tackled near the sideline.

senses.gloss: Moving or facing into the wind; where the wind is coming from.
With is used to show the other people or things present when something happened.
senses.example: I went to school with my brother.

senses.gloss: With is used to describe something added to something else.
senses.example: The cat has a collar with a bell on it.

senses.gloss: With is used to show what thing is used to do something.
senses.example: He hit the nail with a hammer.

senses.gloss: With is used to add more information.
senses.example: With the children so sick, we weren't able to get much work done.

senses.gloss: before the time ends
senses.example: We should be finished within 30 minutes.

senses.gloss: not farther than
senses.example: The station is within a 10 minute walk from here.

senses.gloss: inside
senses.example: He lives within the city walls.

senses.gloss: Used to say that you don't have something or that something isn't in a place.
senses.example: I went all the way to school without my books.

senses.gloss: Used to say that somebody didn't do something or that something didn't happen.
senses.example: She walked past without looking at me.

senses.gloss: outside
If something happens worldwide, it happens throughout the whole world.
senses.example: They travelled worldwide to teach, going to 27 countries.

senses.gloss: A worldwide event or situation happens throughout the whole world.
senses.example: We've seen a worldwide change in how governments are approaching this question.

senses.gloss: Y'all is a combined word meaning "you all".
senses.example: I want to thank y'all for coming.

senses.gloss: Refers to you.
senses.example: Will ya do this for me?

senses.gloss: Your.
senses.example: Where's ya car?

senses.gloss: An old misspelling for the, used especially on pubs' signs or names to make them look old
senses.example: Ye Olde Alcohol Taverne

senses.gloss: An old word for you, but not for one person (plural)
senses.example: Hear ye the news.

senses.gloss: Used to join words, phrases, and clauses that contrast.
senses.example: It's cold outside, yet I don't see you wearing a coat.

senses.gloss: Another way to say "your".
senses.example: Yo' mama's so fat they filmed 'Gorillas in the Mist' in her shower.

senses.gloss: Used before a word that means the person or people someone is talking to.
senses.example: You guys can leave now.

senses.gloss: Used instead of the name of the person being spoken to; it is a second-person pronoun.
senses.example: You are my best friend.

senses.gloss: Used to talk about anyone.
senses.example: You have to be at least 18 years old to see that film.

senses.gloss: You use you all when you emphasize that you are talking to or about more than just one person.
senses.example: I'd like to introduce our new manager to you all.

senses.gloss: When you use you both, you emphasize that you are talking about two people together.
senses.example: I'll give it to you both?

senses.gloss: Your things are things which belong to you.
senses.example: Can I eat some of your ice-cream please?

senses.gloss: If something is yours, it belongs to you.
senses.example: Excuse me, is this yours? I found it on the desk.

senses.gloss: You use yourself when the person doing an action and receiving an action are both you.
senses.example: Be careful. You'll hurt yourself.

senses.gloss: Use for emphasis.
senses.example: Don't pretend you don't know. You yourself have seen these kinds of problems.

senses.gloss: If you do something by yourself, you do it alone.
senses.example: That's beautiful, son. Did you make it by yourself?

senses.gloss: If you don't feel yourself, you are different from usual, because you are sick, scared, stressed, etc.
You use yourselves when the people doing an action and receiving an action are both you.
senses.example: Be careful children. You'll hurt yourselves.

senses.gloss: Use for emphasis.

senses.gloss: If you do something by yourselves, you do it alone.
senses.example: That's beautiful, boys. Did you make it by yourselves?

senses.gloss: If you don't feel yourself, you are different from usual, because you are sick, scared, stressed, etc.
Zero is a count of nothing, the number before one. It is often written 0.
senses.example: It's zero degrees.

senses.gloss: Zero is also used with an uncountable noun.
senses.example: I have zero food left.
